data.title: WEEK: Yield From Weekly Distributions
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Investors seeking exposure to the treasury bill market have several options for doing so in an indexed manner. The Roundhill Weekly T-Bill ETF (WEEK) is one such option, with the particular feature of offering optimization of the payment schedule for the bills it purchases. As the name of the ETF suggests, the fund sacrifices maximization of returns in order to provide weekly payments to its investors, thereby optimizing the aforementioned payment schedule. It is important to make this clear before moving on to the thesis of this analysis, as it will depend greatly on the purposes of each investor. Readers should consider how they manage their liquidity and what their purposes are in managing their cash flow.

As we will see later, the US government is financing itself largely through Treasury bills (short-term bonds). If the government issues more supply, this means that bills become marginally cheaper and short-term yields rise. This is positive for funds that trade in bills, as they are short-term instruments and their interest income (carry) adjusts quickly. As regular readers will know, I generally recommend managing surplus liquidity by investing in this type of short-term instrument. For investors who value weekly cash flow, I recommend buying this vehicle, as there are no other market alternatives that settle carry on a weekly basis (we will look at the drawbacks later). If your goal is to maximize the return on that invested liquidity, then I recommend selling the vehicle and exposing yourself to other alternatives that are more suitable for that purpose, which we will analyze below.

ETF Overview

Looking at the main characteristics of this ETF, we find that it has a 30-day security yield of 3.58% with weekly distribution and a distribution ratio of 3.51%. This in itself does not tell us much, but if we compare it with the current yield on 3-month Treasury bills (see image below), we see that the difference is 0.13% in terms of the distribution rate. Here we can see the effect of the fund's fees (expense ratio of 0.19%) and the bid/ask spread of 0.03%.

If we look at the fund's holdings, we see that all of its assets are invested in treasury bills with different maturities. Each bill in its portfolio has practically the same weight (7.60% - 7.80%).

The Peers

When analyzing the drawbacks of WEEK and proposing some alternatives for investors seeking weekly income distribution, we will focus on WEEK's most similar peers in the market. For this comparison, I have selected the ETFs SGOV, BIL, USFR, and TFLO (the latter two do not purchase bills, but rather have FRNs attached to short-term rates in their portfolios). As can be seen in the first image, in terms of fees, WEEK does not compete with SGOV or BIL (funds in the same category). These have annual expense ratios of 0.09% and 0.14%, respectively. As I said earlier, if you want weekly payments, buy WEEK, but if that doesn't matter so much to you, I would stick with SGOV to park liquidity. In terms of AUM, we can see that WEEK has significantly lower assets under management than SGOV or BIL. This may directly influence a wider bid-ask spread in the case of WEEK, with less market depth.

When we look at the images of price and total returns, we see the following. WEEK is much less “volatile” than other ETFs and performs worse in terms of total returns. 3.47% vs. 4.21% for SGOV. This shows that WEEK's weekly payment management sacrifices some performance. With a higher cost, I still recommend SGOV, if you don't mind not receiving those weekly flows.

T- Bill Treasury Management

Finally, I will focus on the more macroeconomic part of the article and on the statement I have made about why I prefer Treasury bills for managing short-term liquidity. The two images above show the percentage of US sovereign debt issuance relative to the country's GDP throughout the 21st century (first image) and the total percentage of debt issuance by instrument (image 2). As the reader can see, the US Treasury has been issuing Treasury bills throughout all these years until it is now financed mostly by this type of short-term instrument. Specifically, in 2025, most of the issuance by maturity is bills. This directly influences the Treasury bills themselves, lowering their prices and raising their yields. If we add to this a Treasury and a Federal Reserve as structural demanders of bills ( see RMP program), we can say that a Treasury loaded with bills tends to sustain attractive yields at the front end as long as the Fed does not cut aggressively. That is why I like to manage my liquidity with Treasury bills in this macroeconomic environment of slow rate cuts. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Reviewing All My Stocks - December Dividend Income Report
data.description:
In 2016, I made a life-changing decision: I took a sabbatical, put my family in a small RV, and we drove all the way to Costa Rica.

Upon my return in 2017, I officially quit my job as a private banker at National Bank and started working full-time on my baby: Dividend Stocks Rock. I also decided to manage my pension account held at the National Bank. I’ve built and managed this portfolio publicly since 2017 to create and track a real-life case study.

In August 2017, I received $108,760.02 in a locked retirement account. Locked means I can’t add capital to the account, and growth is only generated through capital gains and dividends. I don’t report this portfolio’s results to brag about my returns or to suggest you follow my lead. My purpose was solely to share with our members how I manage my portfolio with all the good and the bad that inevitably takes place each month. I hope you have learned and will continue to learn from my experiences managing this portfolio.

Reviewing All My Stocks 2026

New year, new me, but same old dividend growth strategy! At the start of the year, I thought I would share my expectations and perceptions for each of my pension plan holdings. I significantly underperformed the market in 2025 (7.34% vs. 20.50% for my benchmark). However, it means little as I don’t invest for 12 months, but for 40 years. Since inception, I’m still on track to match the market while not chasing returns. I prefer to have control over my portfolio and be fully confident in my holdings instead of wondering how Mr. Market will react to waking up the next morning. Let’s review each position!

But first, the results!

Performance in Review

Let’s start with the numbers as of January 5th, 2025 (before the bell):

Original amount invested in September 2017 (no additional capital added): $108,760.02.

  • Current portfolio value: $324,300.60
  • Dividends paid: $5,227.59 (TTM)
  • Average yield: 1.61%
  • 2025 performance: +7.34%
  • VFV.TO= +12.18%, XIU.TO = +28.88%
  • Dividend growth: +1.5%

Total return since inception (Sep 2017-November 2025): +198.18%

Annualized return (98 months): 14.31%

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 16.23% (total return 241.80%)

iShares S&P/TSX 60 ETF (XIU:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 12.84% (total return 168.30%)

Dynamic sector allocation calculated by DSR PRO as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

Automatic Data Processing (ADP) – Industrial

ADP is still my favorite “quiet compounding machine”. Payroll is sticky, and the real magic is the mountain of recurring fees that keeps showing up like clockwork. What I expect going forward is steady mid-single-digit growth with margin discipline, plus the usual upside when employment stays healthier than the headlines suggest. The main thing I’m watching is wage and hiring momentum because ADP can feel it in the numbers before economists do. I don’t mind ADP’s poor short-term performance as the dividend triangle metrics are stellar.

Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA) – Consumer Staples

Couche-Tard keeps doing what Couche-Tard does. It turns boring convenience retail sales into a cash engine with discipline and scale. What I expect is continued resilience in margins and cash generation, with management staying opportunistic on tuck-in deals while keeping the balance sheet in check. Let’s be honest, as they are long overdue for announcing a major acquisition! The big swing factor is fuel spreads and FX noise, but the long game is still about traffic, merchandise, and relentless execution.

Broadcom (AVGO) – Information Technology

Broadcom is the newest addition to my portfolio. I’ve been praising this company for 10 years (since 2016) so it’s about time I got a piece of the cake! I love AVGO’s proven growth-by-acquisition strategy. The company enjoys a steady income flow from its RF filter sales (improving smartphone connections), and it has surfed on the AI tailwinds as its chips facilitate faster and less energy-consuming calculations. Even if AI doesn’t turn out into this well of profitability expected by many, everyone needs more efficient computers.

Brookfield Renewable Corp (BEPC:CA) – Utilities

Brookfield Renewable is where I go for the long runway, contracted cash flows plus a developer’s mindset with management that thinks in decades. Ironically, I share the same mindset when it comes to investing, patient investors win. BEPC should continue to thrive if AI is at the center of this bullish narrative. However, I don’t fancy myself thinking price swings are over!

Brookfield Corp (BN:CA) – Financial Services

Brookfield is my “financial octopus” with lots of arms, lots of fee streams, and a talent for buying when others are nervous. What I expect is more focus on fundraising, fee-related earnings, and disciplined deployments tied to power, data, and real assets, especially where AI infrastructure needs real-world shovels. The company owns the entire infrastructure behind the energy demand from the rise of AI. BN owns power plants, renewable energy, power grids, pipelines, real estate, data center and cloud services. They are well-positioned to ride this wave. The stock can trade like a mood ring, but the business is built for patient compounding.

CCL Industries (CCL.B:CA) – Basic Materials

CCL is the kind of company that makes money on things you touch every day, like labels, packaging, and specialty materials, with a surprisingly global footprint. What I expect is continued steady execution and selective bolt-on deals with earnings driven more by operational discipline than heroic forecasts. The risk is that it can feel cyclical when industrial demand cools, even if the long-term story stays intact.

Dollarama (DOL:CA) – Consumer Discretionary

Dollarama is my “inflation pressure valve” stock. When households feel squeezed, the value retail outlet becomes a habit. What I expect is continued strong traffic and same-store sales, plus a long, slow integration story in Australia that will not be linear. DOL is executing its flawless playbook when it comes to expansion: opening a determined number of stores each year (now in Canada, Mexico, Central America and Australia!). The organic growth should continue along the same line. The biggest risk is simply lapping tough comparables and any margin noise from freight or wage pressure, but the model has proven it can adapt.

Fortis (FTS:CA) – Utilities

Fortis is still the portfolio’s seatbelt with regulated assets, visible capex, and a dividend policy that’s designed to be boring in the best way. I like to say that I can put FTS in a black box and open it in 10 years without any worry. What I expect is steady rate base growth and more updates around the multi-year capital plan with modest earnings growth doing the heavy lifting. The main risk is rate sensitivity as utilities can get tossed around when bond yields jump even if the fundamentals barely move.

Granite Real Estate Investment Trust (GRT.UN:CA) – REITs

Granite is my “frustrating investment with good numbers”. At least GRT had a good year in 2025! Nonetheless, I’m roughly breaking even on this investment while my total portfolio is almost up 300%… The REIT owns high quality industrial real estate with a management team that leans conservative. What I expect is continued emphasis on occupancy and rent spreads plus steady distributions that reflect the underlying cash flow discipline. The key risk remains refinancing and cap rates, but Granite has been actively managing through the rate regime.

Home Depot (HD) – Consumer Discretionary

Home Depot is the housing cycle thermometer, and right now that thermometer is reading “slow turnover, cautious homeowners.” What I expect is a gradual thaw as rates eventually ease and projects come back, with Pros and repair and maintenance doing more of the work than big remodel dreams. 2026 should be a better year for the world’s largest home retailer. The risk is that the housing slump lasts longer than anyone wants, and HD can look stuck in place for a while, even if it’s building future demand quietly.

LeMaitre Vascular (LMAT) – Healthcare

LeMaitre is one of my favorite small compounders. It’s not flashy, but it keeps shipping specialized vascular tools into a growing market. I like the niche of selling equipment to surgeons and the robust balance sheet along with double-digit dividend increases! What I expect is continued organic growth with strong profitability, and management staying disciplined on product focus rather than chasing shiny distractions. The risk is always hospital purchasing cycles and procedure volumes, but the recent margin profile shows how powerful the niche can be when the execution is tight.

Microsoft (MSFT) – Information Technology

Microsoft is still the empire of recurring revenue, and AI is becoming an add-on engine across the whole stack. I first bought it for its subscription-based business and its cloud opportunity (Azure). Now the cloud is growing even faster thanks to AI! What I expect is continued Azure momentum and Copilot adoption, with near-term margin pressure as they keep investing heavily in AI infrastructure. The risk is that “capacity and spend” headlines spook the market, but long term, this is exactly how moats get deeper.

National Bank (NA:CA) – Financial Services

National Bank is my favorite Canadian bank. The bank is not just my favorite because I worked there for 13 years, but because it constantly reports strong numbers. It’s always in the top 2 or 3 among the Big 6 in all metrics (revenue growth, EPS growth, dividend growth, dividend payout ratio). The bank acquired Canadian Western Bank in early 2025 and ended the year by acquiring Laurentian Bank’s banking book of business. I expect another year of flawless execution.

Royal Bank (RY:CA) – Financial Services

RBC is Canada’s largest bank and my second favorite behind NA. I like the size, the scale and the diversification (50% of its revenue doesn’t come from classic banking activities). What I expect is continued strength in wealth and capital markets plus the usual focus on credit quality as the economy digests higher rates. The risk is not one bad quarter. It’s the slow creep of credit costs if unemployment rises, but RBC has the balance sheet and earnings power to manage cycles.

Stella-Jones (SJ:CA) – Basic Materials

Stella-Jones is the kind of business that looks dull on the surface, but then you realize rail ties and utility poles are long-lived necessities. What I expect is steady demand in the core categories with pricing and project timing doing most of the quarter-to-quarter dance. The risk is that volume can soften in parts of the cycle, but the “infrastructure replacement” drumbeat usually keeps playing.

Toromont Industries (TIH:CA) – Industrial

Toromont is my “Canada builds things” proxy, and I like having that exposure when the industrial cycle cooperates. For those expecting like me that more investment in infrastructure is coming, TIH should be in a great position to surf this tailwind. What I expect is continued strength from CIMCO and solid product support with equipment sales more dependent on mining and construction timing. The risk is a choppier capex environment, but the aftermarket and service side helps smooth the ride.

Visa Inc (V) – Financial Services

Visa is the toll booth on global commerce, and toll booths do not need to be exciting to be insanely profitable. What I expect is continued growth in payments volume and cross border activity with pricing power showing up through the cycle. The risk is regulatory noise and occasional macro slowdowns, but the cashless trend keeps pushing the river in Visa’s direction.

Waste Connections (WCN:CA) – Industrials

Waste Connections is my “boring, beautiful monopoly by route density”, once you own the pickup routes, the economics get very sticky. What I expect is steady revenue growth, disciplined pricing, and continued shareholder returns, including the dividend growth they’ve been showing. The risk is acquisition integration and commodity volatility around recycling, but the core hauling business tends to be remarkably dependable.

Smith Manoeuvre Update

Slowly but surely, the portfolio is taking shape with 12 companies spread across 7 sectors. My goal is to build a portfolio of thriving companies with a solid dividend triangle (e.g., with positive revenue, EPS and dividend growth trends). The current portfolio yield is at 2.23% with a 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 11.59%.

  • The portfolio value is now at $29,313.61
  • The portfolio debt is at $23,000.
  • Monthly contribution is set at $1,000/month.
  • The annual income is $653.60, and the projected income is $729.33
  • To report my Smith Manoeuvre, I export the Excel data from my DSR PRO dashboard.

Smith Manoeuvre Portfolio Summary

Here’s my SM portfolio summary as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

$1,000 per month starting in January

I will start 2026 by adding $1,000 to some of my smallest positions. For example, I added $1,000 in Great-West (GWO:CA) with my January contribution.

The point will be to balance the portfolio and then move forward. I also have a few stocks in mind to add next year!

Pension Portfolio Summary

Here’s my pension plan portfolio summary as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

Dividend Income: $770.14 (FLAT VS. DECEMBER 2024)

Since I started this portfolio in September 2017, I have received a total of $34,439.59 CAD in dividends. Keep in mind that this is a “pure dividend growth portfolio” as no capital can be added to this account other than retained and/or reinvested dividends. Therefore, all dividend growth is coming from the stocks and not from any additional capital being added to the account.

Final Thoughts

I’m starting strong in 2026 with the increase of my monthly contribution to my Smith Manoeuvre account. I already have over $1,000 in cash in my pension account, thanks to several dividend payments. I’ll be looking to buy more income-producing assets in January!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The One Data Point That Changed My Dividend Growth Strategy
data.description:
When approaching a dividend growth investing strategy, investors typically think that they have to constantly trade off between yield and growth, as well as quality and total return potential. This is typically seen when some investors, especially younger ones, lean heavier into growth while taking a smaller initial yield, thereby looking at companies like Home Depot (HD), Lowe's (LOW), or even some of the dividend-paying mega-cap tech stocks like Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL), which all yield in the very low single digits. Alternatively, some simply invest in low-cost dividend growth ETFs that have this sort of profile, with the likes of the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund (VIG), which pays just a 1.58% trailing 12-month dividend yield.

Meanwhile, on the other end of the spectrum, you can invest in high-yield, slower-growing stocks like blue-chip BDCs (BIZD) like Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) that slowly increase their dividend over time, REITs (VNQ) like Realty Income (O), or midstream infrastructure companies (MLPX) like Enbridge (ENB), as these all offer yields in the mid-to-high single digits and only grow their dividends each year at a low single-digit pace. Then there are those who look for a happy medium and invest in funds like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), which has a trailing 12-month dividend yield of about 3.7% but has managed to grow its dividend at a high single-digit to low teens CAGR in the past.

While this all sounds well and good, I noticed that there is one sector in particular in the market where you can get mid-to-high single-digit yields but also generate strong growth rates that easily beat inflation over time, thanks in large part to being tied to the AI macro tailwinds, as well as getting a boost from the persistently high inflation we’ve been experiencing recently.

To top it off, these companies are generally high quality with investment-grade balance sheets and durable and defensive business models. This realization made me completely change how I think about dividend growth investing and ultimately how I’m allocating the lion’s share of my investment portfolio right now. In this article, I will detail more about this and how it is impacting my investment strategy.

The Rare Sector That Combines Yield, Growth, and Quality

The convergence of unprecedented data center demand for reliable, clean, and affordable power and other related infrastructure investments, as well as recent improvements in infrastructure company balance sheets and their very high yields, are all combining to make AI-related infrastructure (UTF) the most compelling place to invest capital today. This is because it creates a very rare buying opportunity that combines yield, growth, and quality all in a single company. In fact, investors now can get equity-like total returns, bond-like yields and income dependability, and inflation protection all at the same time.

The yield is extremely attractive, especially on a relative basis, as the S&P 500 (SPY) currently yields just a little bit over 1%. While you can buy high-quality infrastructure businesses with a yield that ranges from 5% to 9% today. For example, you can buy Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), which has around a 5% yield at the moment. You can buy the likes of Western Midstream Partners (WES) and Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)(PAGP) that yield near 9%. In between, you can buy many other opportunities like MPLX (MPLX), which is yielding around 8% on a forward-looking basis and growing its distribution at a double-digit annualized pace.

You can also buy Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), which has a near 6% next-12-month yield and is growing its underlying cash flows per unit at a double-digit annualized pace. Across the sector, whether it be in AI infrastructure, renewable and nuclear, renewable nuclear and hydropower infrastructure, or in energy midstream infrastructure, you can get mid-to-high single-digit yields, high-to-low double-digit growth rates, and enjoy that and sleep well at night thanks to having balance sheets that have rock-solid investment-grade credit ratings, well-laddered debt maturities, and highly contracted cash flow profiles.

Not only that, but these distributions are well covered and highly sustainable across economic cycles. In fact, these businesses have phenomenal dividend growth track records in most cases, and some even have very high distribution coverage ratios. For example, Energy Transfer (ET) covers its distribution by nearly 2x, as does ONEOK (OKE). Even some of the tighter coverage ones like BEP are growing rapidly, which makes their forward distribution profile still quite safe, especially when accounting for the strong balance sheets, plenty of liquidity, and highly contracted cash flows.

Why AI and Data Center Demand Are Game-Changers for Infrastructure

Additionally, these businesses are enjoying major macro tailwinds that the market seems to have not fully appreciated yet. While some energy infrastructure plays like Vistra Corp. (VST) and the Williams Companies (WMB) have been bid up substantially due to expected tailwinds for AI data center-related power demand, other energy producers still remain attractively priced despite clear evidence that they are benefiting from AI and power demand.

For example, BEP has already signed two major contracts with hyperscalers in Microsoft and Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), yet its stock price has remained largely range-bound, even as it is putting up strong growth numbers and translating that growth into distribution growth. Another opportunity, OKE, trades near 52-week lows, despite management openly stating that it is competing for dozens of data center natural gas pipeline contracts.

With global data center power demand expected to rise by 165% by 2030 and electricity demand expected to rise by 25% in the U.S. by 2030, with trillions of dollars expected to be spent on AI-related capex, this is a very real tailwind for these companies that the market does not seem to be fully appreciating.

Midstream, Renewables, and the Most Mispriced Income Opportunities

What this means for me is that I am now making AI-related infrastructure and especially energy infrastructure the cornerstone of my portfolio because I am loading up on MLPs (AMLP) as well as other infrastructure plays like BIP and BEP. Another option for investors who want to get broad diversification and is still actively managed by a team that has proven to outperform over the long term is the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), as this provides broad, diversified exposure across the energy infrastructure domain while paying out attractive monthly distributions of well over 6% in a fund that has proven to sustain and grow its dividend over the long term, even through all sorts of economic crises.

Risks to Watch and Why the Long-Term Thesis Still Holds

Of course, no investment is risk-free, and these are not either, as energy infrastructure does remain exposed to operational and project execution risks, as well as regulatory and political risks. Not only that, but the strong expected demand from AI capex could dry up in a hurry if AI investments fail to deliver the returns that were previously expected on capex.

Additionally, midstream companies in particular are somewhat sensitive to energy price volatility, while infrastructure companies like BEP and BIP have also displayed sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations. However, over the long term, I remain bullish on all these opportunities due to their highly contracted cash flows, attractive yields, and overall attractive valuations. That means that even in the event that AI demand underwhelms, they still should deliver reasonably attractive total returns over the long term.

As a result, I believe they are the most attractive risk-adjusted dividend growth plays in the market today, and I am investing heavily in them accordingly in my 7-8%-yielding, low-beta, and outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway Brands Stunning Rise Necessitates A Recalibration
data.description:
2026 is already looking to be a great year for shareholders of Topgolf Callaway Brands (MODG). The stock is currently up 25.1% year to date. And over the past year, it has risen an astounding 79.4%. This surge has been driven by the market finally realizing the potential that the company offers. In my last article about it, published in November 2025, I talked about how the firm offered investors additional upside. This was based not only on valuation but also on a major financial transaction that the company was working on. Specifically at that time, the company was pushing for a transaction that valued its Topgolf business at around $1 billion. After that article was published, management came out with an even larger deal than anticipated. And earlier this month, they closed on that transaction and announced some other developments of note.

Many of the companies that I write about are ones that I do not own shares of. However, I do own a lot of Topgolf Callaway Brands. In fact, because of how much the stock has appreciated as of late, it has ballooned into my largest position, accounting for around a quarter of my total net assets. I don't mind having concentrated portfolios. But even that's a lot for me. Instead of selling off the stock when it is still trading a bit on the cheap, however, I made a different decision entirely. This is a decision that put money into my pocket today, with the only downside being that my returns, which are already stellar, could be capped.

How I’m approaching Topgolf Callaway Brands

I don't like to make things terribly complex when it comes to investing. I think that complexity wastes a lot of mental energy and that the total return associated with that extra effort is rarely worth it. Every so often, however, I do find there to be an exception to the rule. And the picture that we are seeing with Topgolf Callaway Brands is a prime example. Before I get into the specifics of the transactions that I made, however, I believe that touching on what all has gone on since my previous article in November of last year would be appropriate.

For starters, on Jan. 5 of this year, management announced that they completed the sale of the company’s majority ownership stake in Topgolf. Even though I liked Topgolf being part of the mold, it's clear that the market was not enthusiastic. In the years that followed Callaway Brands and Topgolf merging, shares of the combined company had plummeted, even though revenue growth continued. Concerns emerged about the expense of growing Topgolf, and worries began to mount regarding weakness on a same-venue basis. When I originally bought into Topgolf Callaway Brands back in March 2024, I did not expect a breakup of the enterprise. And in fact, I was actually a bit disheartened when management announced their intent to return to their roots.

Late last year, it was expected that Topgolf Callaway Brands would divest of all of Topgolf. This followed an earlier plan to spin off the business into a separate publicly traded enterprise. But at the last minute, instead of selling off Topgolf, the company decided to sell off just part of it. According to the terms of the transaction, Topgolf Callaway Brands had decided to sell off a 60% ownership stake in Topgolf in a transaction valuing the business at $1.1 billion. The firm ended up with $800 million of cash proceeds, with the difference in math here attributable to net cash transfers. This $800 million figure was actually above the $770 million in net cash that the company had previously expected. So that's great in and of itself.

At the end of the most recent fiscal quarter for which we have data available, Topgolf Callaway Brands had $889.5 million in net debt on its books. The company used this transaction, as well as organic cash flows being generated, to wipe out debt completely subsequent to the end of the quarter. I guess it would be more accurate to say that debt outstanding after the transaction closed was $480 million. But the company had $680 million worth of cash, leaving a net cash position of $200 million. The heavy lift here and reducing debt was the repayment of the $1 billion term loan facility that the company had. Ultimately, this reduces risk and will lower interest expense considerably. Management even mentioned earlier this month that they are now focused on a $200 million share buyback program.

This news has all been well received by the market. What we have here is a company that should generate around $2.14 billion in revenue on an annual basis. It has a net cash position, and it also includes an equity stake in Topgolf valued at $513.5 million. As of this writing, Topgolf Callaway Brands has a market capitalization of about $2.69 billion. When we strip out from this the net cash and remove this equity value that is remaining, we should have an assigned valuation on Callaway Brands itself of about $1.97 billion. That translates to an enterprise value to revenue multiple of only 0.92. To put this in perspective, the only real publicly traded competitor of Topgolf Callaway Brands, Acushnet Holdings (GOLF), is trading at an enterprise value to revenue multiple of 2.37.

This is, mind you, after the surge in price that Topgolf Callaway Brands has experienced. However, I would be hesitant to predict that Topgolf Callaway Brands will go up to those multiples. As the chart above illustrates, Acushnet Holdings is doing remarkably well from a valuation standpoint. In fact, using this metric, the business is trading near its all-time high price. This comes after a 26% surge in share price over the last 12 months. Historically speaking, a more realistic range for that company is between 1.5 and 2. And considering my own view that the economy is worsening and the challenges that exist in some markets that are vital for the golf industry, with examples being Japan and South Korea, I would argue that for those who are more conservative like myself, a multiple near the lower end of this range would be desirable.

It was with this in mind that I made a rather interesting move. On Jan. 14, I decided to sell call options covering about 73.4% of my shares that I'd purchased in Topgolf Callaway Brands. I staggered these, with half of the options involving the $17.50 strike calls that expire in January of 2027, and with the remainder involving the $20 calls that expired that same month. Over the time that I purchased shares of Topgolf Callaway Brands, I was able to get my weighted average price down to $7.53. But I also bought a very small long call option position with a $7.50 strike price that expires in July of this year. Already I was up 94% on my stock position. And on my long call option position, I am up 316%.

Selling the call options that I sold on Jan. 14 gave me a return on my initial investment between the shares and options of 16.8%. That's what I get rewarded based on my initial investment for agreeing to cap my upside at what is essentially $20.53 per share. Of course, I still get upside from my remaining units, as well as from my small long call option position. But without any doubt, if things go exceptionally well, I will have taken a short-term but very sizable amount of cash at the cost of even more monumental upside.

In the chart above, you can see, based on implied EV to revenue multiples for Topgolf Callaway Brands, how much upside or downside I will experience because of this decision compared to what my situation would look like without selling these call options. If, for instance, Topgolf Callaway Brands drops to an EV to revenue multiple of only 0.75, my downside would be 32.03%. But without these call options, it would be 39.82%. Where we get to a price that makes sense to me, is where we end up with this strategy that did not include these new options ultimately outperforming the path that I ended up taking. At extreme levels, including under the assumption that Topgolf Callaway Brands could ultimately trade the same multiple as Acushnet Holdings, the additional upside that I am foregoing would be approximately 35.83%.

Having said that, as the chart below illustrates, such an expansion of the trading multiple would still entitle me to a total return on my original initial investment in the company of 224.38%. No, that's not as impressive as the 302.10% that would be captured had I not entered into this strategy. But the extra cash today, and the benefit it gives me in the event that shares trace back lower for any reason, is worth that. I don't need to eke out every penny I possibly can from an opportunity, especially one that has already resulted in significant returns. I have been burned holding out for the last penny in the past. And I would prefer not to do that this time around.

A special note

One thing that deserves to be said is that management did say that, around Jan. 15, the company intends to change its name back to Callaway Golf Company and its ticker to CALY.

Takeaway

Currently, the euphoria that many investors are likely feeling is almost certainly high. Having said that, I believe that taking some profits here might not be a bad idea. I initially considered selling my shares, but I did not want to miss out on more upside that I think is highly likely to materialize. Instead, I took a different route, ultimately selling some call options today to raise a hefty amount of cash that can be deployed elsewhere. This doesn't change my opinion that Topgolf Callaway Brands still deserves the "Strong Buy" rating that I assigned it previously. But it does mean that, on a risk-adjusted basis, I am starting to be more cautious after this historic amount of appreciation.

data.title: ImmunityBio: Why I Am Upgrading To A Buy For 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview

I assigned a Hold rating to ImmunityBio, Inc. (IBRX) stock back in September last year in a note for Seeking Alpha, and prior to that, a Hold rating in April 2025, and a Buy rating in December 2024.

Shares have risen in value by 5% since my Buy call, by 21% since my April Hold call, and by 17% since my last Hold call.

I have described the "triangle offence" qualities of Anktiva, Immunity's lead and only commercial asset, in previous notes, the phrase having been coined by Immunity's majority owner (who owned ~66% of common stock as of the end of Q3, either personally or via affiliates), Dr Soon Shiong, a billionaire and minority owner of the LA lakers basketball team.

To summarize, as per Immunity's latest 10-Q filing:

ANKTIVA is a first-in-class IL-15 receptor superagonist IgG1 fusion complex, consisting of an IL-15 mutant (IL-15N72D) fused with an IL-15 receptor alpha, which binds with high affinity to IL-15 receptors on NK, CD4+, and CD8+ T cells.

As I wrote in my last note:

Anktiva was approved in April 2024, in the indication of BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer ("NMIBC"), based on a clinical study which showed a complete response ("CR") rate of 62% in 77 evaluable patients, with a complete duration of response ("DoR") rate of more than 47 months.

More recent data has suggested a 71% complete response ("CR") rate (source: corporate presentation), with DoR of >47 months, which is far superior to two other therapies approved in the same indication, Merck's (MRK) checkpoint inhibitor Keytruda, DoR 16.2m, and Ferring Pharmaceuticals' gene therapy Adstiladrin, DoR of 9.7m.

Anktiva has an acceptable safety profile, with a 7% treatment discontinuation rate ("serious adverse reactions occurred in 16% of patients receiving Anktiva with BCG"), but to date, commercial success has proven elusive.

In the first three quarters of 2025, Immunity reported product revenues of $74.7m, and today, management released a preliminary product revenue figure of $113m - up ~700% year-on-year, but up just 20% sequentially, the Q4 revenue figure being $38.3m.

Other positives shared in today's update are that unit sales volume growth in 2025 as compared to 2024 was up 750%, and that the drug has been approved in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, both for NMIBC, and for adults with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC"), which represents the first approval for the drug in that indication, and the first approval of a subcutaneous version of the drug.

As I wrote in my last note:

Immunity hopes to secure further approvals for Anktiva in indications such as lung, colon, prostate, and heme cancers, and it's been speculated the therapy could achieve close to $900m in peak annual revenues, which, if true, suggests that Immunity's business is undervalued based on its current share price of $2.6 per share.

Immunity's current market cap is $3.4bn, and I continue to believe the investment case is finely balanced. On the one hand, approvals in first line bladder, NSCLC, plus other indications may see peak revenue expectations raised considerably, and there is data available to suggest these approvals are likely.

On the other hand, Immunity's accumulated deficit now stands at >$3.6bn, while the company made a nine-month net loss in 2025 of $(289.5m), and reported a cash position of $258m - will the company ever achieve profitability, and in the meantime, will its founder keep pumping money into the business?

In this post, I'll assess the company's prospects for 2026.

Immunity In 2026 - Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats

In a January 2025 investor presentation, Immunity suggests that Anktiva can become "the backbone of immunotherapy 2.0, beyond checkpoint inhibitors," as shown in the slide below:

Checkpoint inhibitors such as the PD-1 targeting Keytruda - which will earn >$30bn of revenues in 2025, and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Opdivo, which will likely earn >$10bn, have certainly formed a treatment "backbone" for solid tumor cancer for many years, while the likes of Rituxan and CAR-T cell therapies like BMY's Abedma and Breyanzi have earned billions of dollars of revenue treating heme cancers.

As we can see above, Immunity has ambitious plans to partner with all three types of treatment, opening up massive market opportunities, especially in lung cancer, the largest of all oncology markets.

A couple of days ago, Immunity announced that Anktiva plus, a checkpoint inhibitor:

demonstrated statistically significant immune restoration across two clinical trials in 151 patients with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC)

  • In first-line NSCLC (QUILT-2.023), a randomized study demonstrated a statistically significant and sustained increase in absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) from baseline with ANKTIVA plus CPI compared with CPI alone (p=0.0065), establishing ANKTIVA’s contribution as a lymphocyte-stimulating agent.
  • In second- and later-line NSCLC (QUILT-3.055), a single-arm study demonstrated restoration or maintenance of ALC ≥1.0 × 10³ cells/µL in 77% of patients receiving ANKTIVA plus CPI (responders).
  • Responders receiving ANKTIVA plus CPI experienced significantly longer overall survival compared with non-responders (median OS 16.2 vs 11.8 months; HR 0.52; p=0.0369), directly linking immune restoration to clinical benefit. Patients achieving higher immune competence (ALC ≥1.2 ×10³ cells/µL) demonstrated additional survival benefit, with median OS of 21.1 months (HR 0.33; p=0.0009), independent of PD-L1 status, exceeding historical overall survival of 7-9 months with standard of care chemotherapy.

While I would stop short of suggesting this data makes Anktiva's approval in lung cancer a formality, it seemingly supports Immunity's thesis that it could be a superior treatment for patients relapsing after treatment with an immune checkpoint inhibitor ("ICI") than chemotherapy, which is the current standard-of-care ("SoC"). In its investor presentation, Immunity states that patients whose cancer progresses after ICI therapy have an expected overall survival of <10 months.

Immunity says in the same presentation it expected to submit a biologics license application ("BLA") requesting FDA approval in 2nd / 3rd line NSCLC, based on its QUILT 3.055 study in 2025. In that study, a median OS of 14.1 months has been observed, with 24 patients still on the study. Long-term survival was 33.7% at ≥18 months and 31.4% ≥21 months.

So far as I am aware, that has not happened, but according to its Q3 10Q filing, Immunity has now initiated enrollment in ResQ201A, a global, randomized Phase 3 study evaluating ANKTIVA in combination with TEVIMBRA (BeOne) and docetaxel versus docetaxel alone in patients with checkpoint inhibitor-resistant NSCLC, and a BLA submission is still planned.

Certainly, the recent positive news-flow has had a positive effect on Immunity's share price, which is up 48% across the past 5 days.

Meanwhile, Immunity's attempts to secure a label expansion in bladder cancer received a boost in December. A study in patients with BCG-unresponsive high-grade papillary-only NMIBC showed a disease-free survival ("DFS") rate of 58% at one-year, and 38% at three-years, while disease-specific survival ("DSS") rates were 98.7%, and 96% respectively. 82% did not require a cystectomy (bladder removal procedure) at three years.

In March last year, the FDA refused to accept Immunity's supplemental BLA requesting approval in this indication, however armed with this latest data, perhaps a new submission may be accepted this year.

In summary, then, it seems Immunity has already taken some positive steps forward in 2026, and could take several more. However, readers should be aware of a new competitive threat in the form of Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) recently approved bladder cancer drug Inlexzo, which achieved an 82% CR rate in its pivotal study.

According to S&P Global, Inlexzo is destined to become the market leader in NMIBC, with a projected revenue figure of $4.7bn by 2035, although Anktiva is assigned a revenue figure of $3.1bn in the same year.

Concluding Thoughts: Revenues, Losses, & Pipeline Progress Will Dictate Share Price Performance In 2026 - I'm Optimistic

In this post, we have looked at Anktiva's improving, if not spectacularly so, commercial performance, some recent positive news-flow, including a first approval in NSCLC, opportunities for label expansions in NMIBC and NSCLC, the ability to partner Anktiva with other therapeutic modalities, new competitive threats, and long-term revenue projections.

Clearly, if we take the 2025 revenue number of $113m and the heavy losses, Immunity would not appear to justify its $3.4bn valuation, but if we think about the long term, i.e., a projected revenue figure higher than today's market cap, we are looking at a different story.

I think a challenging year lies ahead for Immunity, particularly with the arrival of Inlexzo on the scene, which could lead to underwhelming commercial performance. However, the advances being made in other fields, most notably papillary NMIBC and NSCLC, just pushes the needle far enough toward a Buy signal for me to upgrade my rating on Immunity stock for 2026.

This won't be the year Anktiva turns a profit - far from it, but it could be the year data begins to justify its founders' faith in the "Triangle Offence," and the market begins to buy in anticipation of the advent of a new "backbone therapy" in oncology.

data.title: Power Demand Redefines Global Resources
data.description:
AI-driven power demand and electrification are reshaping resource markets as commodities diverge, metals outperform, energy lags and supply constraints return to focus.

Quarterly insights from Global Resources Portfolio Manager Shawn Reynolds, featuring his unique views on natural resources and commodities.

Commodities Diverge as Power Demand Accelerates

Risk appetite held up into year-end, supported by sustained enthusiasm for AI-related capex and expectations that policy rates could drift lower over time. At the same time, trade policy uncertainty and tariff dynamics remained recurring drivers of commodity price dislocations, most visibly in oil and copper.

Resource equities split along commodity lines. Metals and mining equities outperformed on strong copper and precious metals price action, while energy equities lagged as crude’s downtrend and oversupply narrative weighed on confidence in forward earnings and capital allocation.

Sector Performance Recap

Oil & Gas

  • 2025 was a notably weak year for crude oil, with WTI down ~19% (its largest annual decline since 2020). The fourth quarter largely reflected the same pressure points seen earlier in the year, ample supply versus only modest demand, keeping rallies short-lived despite periodic geopolitical headlines.
  • Natural gas remained weather-sensitive in the near term, but the structural demand narrative strengthened, as the U.S. surpassed 100 million metric tons of liquified natural gas exports for the first time, supported by new capacity and sustained European demand. By the fourth quarter, gas was increasingly framed as a feedstock for global energy security, rather than solely a domestic heating and power fuel.
  • With crude prices range-bound, capital discipline and durable return frameworks remained central to upstream positioning, favoring balance-sheet strength over growth.
  • Gas-levered E&Ps and midstream operators benefited from firmer pricing and the AI-related power demand narrative linking gas supply to data center and grid infrastructure buildout.

Renewables & Alternatives

  • Renewable energy delivered a stronger-than-expected fourth quarter and 2025 overall, particularly as financing conditions began to stabilize.
  • The key macro driver remained the cost of capital: developers and yield-sensitive infrastructure improved as rate pressures eased, while utilities increasingly traded on load growth from data centers and the scale of required grid investment.
  • Clean power procurement accelerated, with hyperscalers continuing to pull forward demand through long-term, structured supply arrangements.

Base & Industrial Metals

  • Copper was the standout cyclical bellwether, pushing to new highs in December amid tariff-driven trade distortions, mine disruptions, supply constraints and a demand narrative increasingly tied to electrification and AI-driven data center expansion.
  • Copper producers and diversified miners traded on leverage to underlying prices, supported by solid project execution and longer-term supply scarcity driven by long lead times, permitting challenges and capital intensity across new supply.
  • Steel markets were more mixed, with prices largely range-bound amid ample capacity and uneven global demand. Steel producers emphasized cost control, balance-sheet discipline and trade protection in the face of ongoing import pressure.

Gold & Precious Metals

  • Gold extended its 2025 rally into year-end, reaching new record highs in late December, supported by geopolitical risk, sustained central bank demand, ETF inflows and expectations for future Fed easing.
  • Price action remained resilient, with the market consistently treating pullbacks as buying opportunities, reflecting late-cycle behavior as real-rate expectations softened and macro uncertainty persisted.
  • Producers broadly benefited from the stronger price tape, though dispersion remained driven by cost inflation, mine sequencing, jurisdictional exposure and capital allocation decisions.
  • Precious metals equities re-rated quickly in Q4, reinforcing their sensitivity to policy expectations and liquidity conditions rather than purely geopolitical developments.

Agriculture

  • The 2025 agricultural backdrop was defined by weak pricing and farm income stress, prompting the USDA to announce a $12B aid package in early December aimed at offsetting losses tied to low crop prices and trade disruptions.
  • Processors and traders faced a challenging fourth quarter, with weak crush margins, cautious policy signals and less favorable selling conditions.
  • Ag input suppliers showed mixed performance: fertilizer markets were relatively constructive on firmer pricing and security-of-supply narratives, while crop protection remained pressured by affordability constraints.

Paper & Forest Products

  • Pricing remained soft across forest products in the fourth quarter, with lumber largely range-bound after summer weakness and pulp prices still under pressure following their mid-year peak. End markets such as housing and packaging stabilized but did not meaningfully recover.
  • Supply discipline dominated industry behavior, with curtailments, mill closures and restructuring actions aimed at rebalancing markets and protecting cash flow.
  • Equity performance reflected macro sensitivity, as timber REITs lagged on housing and rate concerns, while private timberland transactions and strategic M&A highlighted long-term asset value despite cyclical weakness.

Portfolio Performance: Drivers and Detractors

Year-to-date, Global Resources Fund (Class A; excluding fees and expenses, the “Fund”) continued to generate strong absolute and relative performance, returning 36.11% versus 28.86% for the S&P Global Natural Resources Index to round out the year. In the fourth quarter, the Fund returned 6.52%, compared with 6.71% for the Index. On an absolute basis, returns were driven primarily by exposure to Gold & Precious Metals, Base & Industrial Metals and Oil & Gas. On a relative basis, Gold & Precious Metals, Renewables & Alternatives and Industrials & Utilities were the largest contributors, while Agriculture, Oil & Gas and Base & Industrial Metals detracted.

Top absolute contributors included Gold & Precious Metals companies Barrick Mining (B) (3.65% of Fund assets) and Pan American Silver (PAAS) (2.10% of Fund assets), both of which benefited from sustained rallies in gold and silver prices, as well as aluminum producer Alcoa (AA) (1.21% of Fund assets). Alcoa outperformed on the back of strong aluminum price appreciation, tight supply-demand fundamentals and rising demand expectations tied to power infrastructure and AI-related electrification.

Top detractors included fertilizer producers Mosaic (MOS) (1.29% of Fund assets) and FMC Corp. (FMC) (not held), as well as Gold & Precious Metals streaming and royalty company Franco-Nevada (FNV) (2.11% of Fund assets). Fertilizer producers detracted amid weak farm economics, lower crop prices and concerns around new supply additions, while Franco-Nevada was negatively impacted by valuation sensitivity to interest rates and company-specific production and guidance headwinds.

Notable Adds

The recent acquisition of a containerboard business should add capacity, enhance vertical integration and meaningfully expand margins as synergies are realized. This operational momentum underpins our favorable outlook for 2026.
Smurfit WestRock (SW) Paper & Forest 1.26 Packaging company serving food & beverage, e-commerce and industrial end markets. Formed through the merger of two industry leaders, the company represents a compelling self-help opportunity. While integration was initially challenging, we expect improving execution as efficiency gains are captured through network optimization, mill rationalization and procurement savings, supporting solid earnings growth and margin expansion.
International Paper (IP) Paper & Forest 1.01 Global leader in packaging solutions and the world’s largest pulp and paper company. A recent acquisition strengthened its footprint across North American and EMEA packaging markets. We expect attractive earnings growth as the company integrates the acquisition, streamlines its portfolio and continues shifting toward higher-margin packaging and containerboard operations.

Notable Exits

We believe this is unlikely to change until North American beer demand recovers to pre-COVID levels.
Rio Tinto (RIO) Base & Indust. Mtls. (Not held) Exited the position amid growing concerns related to the ramp-up and ownership structure of the Simandou iron ore project, as well as ongoing bribery allegations in Mongolia.

Outlook

Heading into 2026, natural resources are being shaped by a dominant force: a structural power crunch. Global electricity demand is rising at its fastest pace in decades as AI data centers, widespread electrification, manufacturing re-shoring and ongoing urbanization drive unprecedented load growth. This wave of demand is colliding with energy systems built for a different era: security of resource supply, insufficient generation capacity, aging transmission networks and disruptive supply chains that are increasingly vulnerable to geopolitical pressure.

Energy availability and affordability have shifted from technical considerations to strategic determinants of economic competitiveness. Nations are now racing to secure fuels, critical minerals, grid equipment and next-generation power technologies. At the same time, multiple years of underinvestment across both energy and materials have created tight supply conditions in several markets, from natural gas to copper and other transition metals.

While policy uncertainty, interest-rate paths, China’s growth trajectory and geopolitical tensions may heighten volatility, the broader setup remains supportive. Secular demand growth from electrification, grid expansion and data-center build-out intersects with slow, complex supply responses - particularly in mining, where multi-year permitting cycles and rising project costs constrain new production. Taken together, these forces underpin a constructive long-term outlook for natural resource equities.

The most compelling investment opportunities emerge where structural demand growth meets constrained supply.

In energy, natural gas remains a critical bridging fuel as grids struggle to accommodate accelerating load growth. Producers with low break-evens, disciplined capital allocation and well-positioned infrastructure continue to benefit from resilient demand patterns. Select oil and integrated energy companies also remain attractive given steady product margins, strong free-cash-flow generation and ongoing portfolio optimization. U.S. output has been responsible for almost all the global supply growth over the last 15 years. This will not be the case in 2026 and beyond and new sources of production will need to be found.

In metals and mining, copper is especially well positioned. Supply disruptions, limited project pipelines and long development timelines are intersecting with rising demand from EVs, grid investment and digital infrastructure. Companies with high-quality assets, clean balance sheets and visible production growth are poised to benefit from these durable trends.

Beyond traditional resource sectors, next-generation power technologies, including advanced nuclear, geothermal, hydrogen systems, long-duration energy storage and AI-optimized grid solutions, represent emerging areas of investment as countries pursue secure, scalable and affordable power.

Across the natural resources landscape, valuations remain attractive, cash generation is robust and secular tailwinds are strengthening. For long-term investors, opportunities lie in owning companies positioned to supply, enable, or secure the world's rapidly evolving power systems.

Geopolitical Implications

Geopolitical risk insurance is a core tenet of VanEck’s Global Resources Fund, and recent developments in Venezuela serve as a timely reminder of why exposure to natural resource equities remains an important component of a diversified investment portfolio. Whether it is Venezuela today, Syria and Nigeria last month, Iran over the summer or countless other flashpoints over the past several decades, geopolitical turbulence has consistently influenced the availability, affordability and security of natural resources and the companies that produce them. These factors are foundational to economic stability, industrial activity and national defense.

Venezuela, in particular, underscores that geopolitical risk is neither confined to the Middle East nor limited to the periphery of the former Soviet sphere. It also highlights how geopolitical events are rarely one-dimensional, often reflecting layered national and strategic priorities. The capture of Nicolás Maduro and subsequent arrangements to redirect Venezuelan crude toward U.S. markets demonstrate how quickly geopolitics can reshape traditional supply-and-demand dynamics, alter market expectations and influence investor sentiment.

These developments further reinforce the value of active management. Shifting geopolitical regimes inevitably create both winners and losers and an active investment approach is better positioned to respond to evolving fundamentals than passive, beta-oriented strategies. In this instance, Venezuela has once again illustrated how rapidly conditions can change and why flexibility matters.

Ultimately, this episode highlights the importance of incorporating geopolitical risk as one of the four pillars of our investment framework for this strategy: inflation protection, leverage to global growth, diversification and geopolitical risk insurance. While today’s environment may feel unusually uncertain, such conditions are not the exception, they are the norm.
